are [grants](/wiki/Grant_(money) "Grant (money)") that have been awarded annually since 1925 (1925) by the **John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation**, endowed by [Simon](/wiki/Simon_Guggenheim "Simon Guggenheim") and Olga Hirsh Guggenheim.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) These awards are bestowed upon individuals who have demonstrated distinguished accomplishment in the past and potential for future achievement. The recipients exhibit outstanding aptitude for prolific scholarship or exceptional talent in the arts.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Grant in the arts, awarded annually

Not to be confused with the [Daniel and Florence Guggenheim Fellowship](/wiki/National_Air_and_Space_Museum#Fellowships "National Air and Space Museum") (Smithsonian Institution) or the defunct [Guggenheim International Award](/wiki/Guggenheim_International_Award "Guggenheim International Award").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(April 2022)* |

**Guggenheim Fellowships** are [grants](/wiki/Grant_(money) "Grant (money)") that have been awarded annually since 1925 (1925) by the **John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation**, endowed by [Simon](/wiki/Simon_Guggenheim "Simon Guggenheim") and Olga Hirsh Guggenheim.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) These awards are bestowed upon individuals who have demonstrated distinguished accomplishment in the past and potential for future achievement. The recipients exhibit outstanding aptitude for prolific scholarship or exceptional talent in the arts.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The foundation holds two separate competitions each year:[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)

* One open to citizens and permanent residents of the United States and Canada.
* The other to citizens and permanent residents of Latin America and the Caribbean. The Latin America and Caribbean competition is currently suspended "while we examine the workings and efficacy of the program. The U.S. and Canadian competition is unaffected by this suspension."[[6]](#cite_note-how-apply-6)

The performing arts are excluded from these fellowships, but composers, film directors, and choreographers are still eligible to apply. While students are not qualified to apply, advanced professionals in mid-career, such as published authors, are encouraged to do so. Upon receipt of the grant, Fellows are free to use the funds however they deem fit.[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) The goal of the grant is to provide recipients with dedicated time and freedom to pursue their projects or artistic endeavours, while being relieved of their regular duties.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Applicants are required to submit references as well as a [CV](/wiki/Curriculum_vitae "Curriculum vitae") and [portfolio](/wiki/Career_portfolio "Career portfolio").

As of 2025, the Guggenheim Memorial Foundation has funded over 19,000 Fellows with a total sum of almost $400 million since its inception.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-gf-15apr2025-11) Each year, the foundation receives a high number of applications; since its formation it has seen anywhere between 500 and 4,000 applications.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) Out of these, approximately 175 Fellowships are awarded.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The size of each grant varies and the amount and duration of the grant is adjusted based on the individual needs of the recipients,[[13]](#cite_note-13) taking into consideration their other resources and the purpose and scope of their plans. The average grant awarded is between $40,000 and $55,000.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guggenheim_Fellowship&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation")]

John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Formation | 1925; 101 years ago (1925) |
| Founder | * Olga Guggenheim * [Simon Guggenheim](/wiki/Simon_Guggenheim "Simon Guggenheim") |
| Headquarters | [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), U.S. |
| President | [Edward Hirsch](/wiki/Edward_Hirsch "Edward Hirsch") |
| Website | [gf.org](http://gf.org) |

The John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation is a [private foundation](/wiki/Private_foundation "Private foundation") formed in 1925[[16]](#cite_note-bloomberg-16) by Olga and [Simon Guggenheim](/wiki/Simon_Guggenheim "Simon Guggenheim") in memory of their son, who died on April 26, 1922.[[17]](#cite_note-17) In 1924, Senator Guggenheim appointed Henry Allen Moe to oversee the creation of the foundation and to lead its first years. The Foundation was officially chartered and incorporated by New York State and Governor [Alfred E. Smith](/wiki/Alfred_E._Smith "Alfred E. Smith") on March 16, 1925.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The organization awards Guggenheim Fellowships to professionals who have demonstrated exceptional ability by publishing a significant body of work in the fields of natural sciences, social sciences, humanities, and the creative arts, excluding the performing arts.[[16]](#cite_note-bloomberg-16)

In 2025, the Foundation announced their appointment of the 100th class of Guggenheim Fellows, including 198 individuals working across 53 disciplines.[[11]](#cite_note-gf-15apr2025-11) To commemorate the centennial year of the Foundation, a special exhibit, *The Guggenheim Fellowship at 100*, was mounted at the New York Historical Museum. The exhibit, opened August 29, 2025 to run through November 30, 2025, explores the rarely seen archives of the Foundation and highlights some of the most notable Guggenheim Fellows of the last century.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## University affiliations of Guggenheim fellows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guggenheim_Fellowship&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: University affiliations of Guggenheim fellows")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Stephen_Vincent_Ben%C3%A9t_Yale_College_BA_1919.jpg/250px-Stephen_Vincent_Ben%C3%A9t_Yale_College_BA_1919.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stephen_Vincent_Ben%C3%A9t_Yale_College_BA_1919.jpg)

[Pulitzer Prize for Poetry](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Poetry "Pulitzer Prize for Poetry")-winning poet and writer [Stephen Vincent Benét](/wiki/Stephen_Vincent_Ben%C3%A9t "Stephen Vincent Benét") authored *[John Brown's Body](/wiki/John_Brown%27s_Body_(poem) "John Brown's Body (poem)")* as a Guggenheim Fellow in Paris in 1926

Since the inaugural class of 1925, over 19,000 fellowships have been awarded.[[11]](#cite_note-gf-15apr2025-11) [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") counts the most affiliated fellows at 176, followed by [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") and [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") in 2nd and 3rd place, respectively.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

| Institution | Fellows (1925-2022) |
| --- | --- |
| [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University")† | 176 |
| [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") | 102 |
| [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") | 96 |
| [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley") | 73 |
| [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University")† | 72 |
| [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") | 65 |
| [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago") | 64 |
| [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University") | 54 |
| [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") | 51 |
| [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan") | 41 |

† Harvard includes [Radcliffe](/wiki/Radcliffe_College "Radcliffe College") and Columbia includes [Barnard College](/wiki/Barnard_College "Barnard College")
